1.didYouKnow: The orange is the fruit of the citrus species Citrus x sinensis in the family Rutaceae. The orange is a hybrid, possibly between pomelo and mandarin, which has been cultivated since ancient times.

2.didYouKnow: A grape is a fruiting berry of the deciduous woody vines of the botanical genus Vitis. Grapes can be eaten raw or they can be used for making wine, jam, juice, jelly, grape seed extract, raisins, vinegar, and grape seed oil.

3.didYouKnow: Paprika is a spice made from air-dried fruits of the chili pepper family of the species Capsicum annuum. Although paprika is often associated with Hungarian cuisine, the chilies from which it is made are native to the New World.

4.didYouKnow: Lavandula (common name lavender) is a genus of 39 known species of flowering plants in the mint family, Lamiaceae. It is native to the Old World and is found from Cape Verde and the Canary Islands, southern Europe across to northern and eastern Africa, the Mediterranean, southwest Asia to southeast India.

5.didYouKnow: Lettuce (Lactuca sativa) is an annual plant of the aster or sunflower family Asteraceae. Lettuce was first cultivated by the ancient Egyptians who turned it from a weed, whose seeds were used to produce oil, into a plant grown for its seeds and leaves.

6.didYouKnow: The carrot (Daucus carota) is a root vegetable, usually orange in color, though purple, red, white, and yellow varieties exist. The domestic carrot has been selectively bred for its greatly enlarged and more palatable, less woody-textured edible taproot.

7.didYouKnow: The olive (Olea europaea, meaning 'olive from/of Europe') is a species of small tree in the family Oleaceae. Its fruit, also called the olive, is of major agricultural importance in the Mediterranean region as the source of olive oil.

8.didYouKnow: Black pepper (Piper nigrum) is a flowering vine in the family Piperaceae, cultivated for its fruit, which is usually dried and used as a spice and seasoning. Black pepper is native to south India, and is extensively cultivated there and elsewhere in tropical regions.

9.didYouKnow: An apple is the pomaceous fruit of the apple tree, Malus domestica of the rose family (Rosaceae). Apples grow on deciduous trees which are large if grown from seed, but small if grafted onto roots (rootstock). The tree originated in Central Asia, where its wild ancestor, Malus sieversii, is still found today.

10.didYouKnow: The lemon (Citrus x limon) is a small evergreen tree native to Asia. The tree's ellipsoidal yellow fruit is used for culinary and non-culinary purposes throughout the world, primarily for its juice, though the pulp and rind (zest) are also used in cooking and baking.

11.didYouKnow: Gysahl Greens are unique to Final Fantasy and can be used to summon Chocobos into battle. It's rumored the greens are a favorite among the Fat Chocobos.

12.didYouKnow: A plum is a drupe fruit of the subgenus Prunus of the genus Prunus. The subgenus is distinguished from other subgenera (peaches, cherries, bird cherries, etc.) in the shoots having a terminal bud and solitary side buds (not clustered).

13.didYouKnow: Mustard plants are any of several plant species in the genera Brassica and Sinapis. Mustard seed is used as a spice. Grinding and mixing the seeds with water, vinegar, or other liquids, creates the yellow condiment known as prepared mustard.

14.didYouKnow: Chamomile or camomile is the common name for several daisy-like plants of the family Asteraceae that are commonly used to make a herb infusion that can help to induce sleep.

15.didYouKnow: Maize, known in some English-speaking countries as corn, is a large grain plant domesticated by indigenous peoples in Mesoamerica in prehistoric times. The leafy stalk produces ears which contain the grain, which are seeds called kernels. Maize kernels are often used in cooking as a starch.

16.didYouKnow: Eggplant (Solanum melongena) is a species of nightshade commonly known in British English as aubergine and also known as melongene, garden egg, or guinea squash. As a member of the genus Solanum, it is related to both the tomato and the potato.

17.didYouKnow: Cabbage (Brassica oleracea or variants) is a leafy green or purple biennial plant, grown as an annual vegetable crop for its dense-leaved heads. Closely related to other cole crops, such as broccoli, cauliflower, and brussels sprouts, it descends from B. oleracea var. oleracea, a wild field cabbage.

18.didYouKnow: Flax (also known as linseed), with the binomial name: Linum usitatissimum, is a member of the genus Linum in the family Linaceae. The plant species is known only as a cultivated plant and appears to have been domesticated just once from the wild species Linum bienne, called pale flax.

19.didYouKnow: Basil, Thai basil, or sweet basil, is a common name for the culinary herb Ocimum basilicum of the family Lamiaceae (mints), sometimes known as Saint Joseph's Wort in some English-speaking countries. Basil is native to India, China, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea. It was originally domesticated in India, having been cultivated there for more than 5,000 years.

20.didYouKnow: Mandrake is the common name for members of the plant genus Mandragora, particularly the species Mandragora officinarum, belonging to the nightshades family. Because mandrake contains deliriant hallucinogenic tropane alkaloids, their roots have long been used in magic rituals, today also in contemporary pagan traditions such as Wicca and Odinism.

21.didYouKnow: Ribes is a genus of about 150 species of flowering plants native throughout the temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere. The genus Ribes includes the edible currants (blackcurrant, redcurrant, white currant), gooseberry, and several hybrid varieties.

22.didYouKnow: The almond is a species of tree native to the Middle East and South Asia. 'Almond' is also the name of the edible and widely cultivated seed of this tree. Within the genus Prunus, it is classified with the peach in the subgenus Amygdalus, distinguished from the other subgenera by the corrugated shell (endocarp) surrounding the seed.

23.didYouKnow: An apple is the pomaceous fruit of the apple tree, Malus domestica of the rose family (Rosaceae). Apples grow on deciduous trees which are large if grown from seed, but small if grafted onto roots (rootstock). The tree originated in Central Asia, where its wild ancestor, Malus sieversii, is still found today.

24.didYouKnow: The garden strawberry (or simply strawberry; Fragaria x ananassa) is a widely grown hybrid species of the genus Fragaria (collectively known as the strawberries). The garden strawberry was first bred in Brittany, France, in the 1750s via a cross of Fragaria virginiana from eastern North America and Fragaria chiloensis, which was brought from Chile

25.didYouKnow: The lemon (Citrus x limon) is a small evergreen tree native to Asia. The tree's ellipsoidal yellow fruit is used for culinary and non-culinary purposes throughout the world, primarily for its juice, though the pulp and rind (zest) are also used in cooking and baking.

26.didYouKnow: The tomato is the edible, often red fruit/berry of the nightshade Solanum lycopersicum, commonly known as a tomato plant. The species originated in the South American Andes[2] and its use as a food originated in Mexico, and spread throughout the world following the Spanish colonization of the Americas.

27.didYouKnow: The pineapple (Ananas comosus) is a tropical plant with edible multiple fruit consisting of coalesced berries, and the most economically significant plant in the Bromeliaceae family. Pineapples may be cultivated from a crown cutting of the fruit, possibly flowering in 20-24 months and fruiting in the following six months.

28.didYouKnow: An apricot is a fruit or the tree that bears the fruit. Usually, an apricot tree is from the tree species Prunus armeniaca, but the species Prunus brigantina, Prunus mandshurica, Prunus mume, and Prunus sibirica are closely related, have similar fruit, and are also called apricots.

29.didYouKnow: Bean is a common name for large plant seeds used for human food or animal feed of several genera of the family Fabaceae. Royal Kukuru beans are unique to Final Fantasy and are prized for their ability to produce Glazenuts when crossed with Apricots.

30.didYouKnow: Tea is an aromatic beverage commonly prepared by pouring hot or boiling water over cured leaves of the tea plant, Camellia sinensis. After water, tea is the most widely consumed beverage in the world. Tea originated in China as a medicinal drink. It was first introduced to Portuguese priests and merchants in China during the 16th century.

31.didYouKnow: A parsnip-like tuber found growing on the wind-swept islands of the northern seas. While too bitter for most people's tastes, chocobos are known to enjoy the flavor, and thus the root is often used as fodder for the animals. Increases EXP earned by your chocobo companion.

32.didYouKnow: A gourd is a plant of the family Cucurbitaceae or the fruit of the two genera of 'calabash tree', Crescentia and Amphitecna. Increases your chocobo companion's healing magic potency.

33.didYouKnow: A bright red tomato-like fruit brought back from the New World by Lominsan explorers. While too tangy for most people's tastes, chocobos are known to enjoy the flavor, and thus the fruits are often used as fodder for the animals. Increases your chocobo companion's enmity.

34.didYouKnow: A rose is a woody perennial of the genus Rosa, within the family Rosaceae. Most species are native to Asia, with smaller numbers native to Europe, North America, and northwest Africa.

35.didYouKnow: Illicium verum, commonly called star anise, is a spice that closely resembles anise in flavor, obtained from the star-shaped pericarp of Illicium verum, a medium-sized native evergreen tree of northeast Vietnam and southwest China.

36.didYouKnow: The leek is a vegetable that belongs, along with onion and garlic, to the genus Allium, currently placed in family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Allioideae. The edible part of the leek plant is a bundle of leaf sheaths that is sometimes erroneously called a stem or stalk.

37.didYouKnow: Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) is a species of Hibiscus native to the west Africa, used for the production of bast fiber and as an infusion, in which it may also be known as carcade. Hibiscus, specifically roselle, has been used in folk medicine as a diuretic, mild laxative, and treatment for cardiac and nerve diseases and cancer.

38.didYouKnow: An artichoke-like flower found growing on a majority of the Cieldalaes. While too bitter for most people's tastes, chocobos are known to enjoy the flavor, and thus the meaty buds are often used as fodder for the animals. Increases your chocobo companion's attack potency.

39.didYouKnow: A vibrant purple vegetable commonly found growing in the Near East. While too bitter for most people's tastes, chocobos are known to enjoy the flavor, and thus these aubergines are often used as fodder for the animals. Increases your chocobo companion's maximum HP.

40.didYouKnow: Gerbera L. is a genus of ornamental plants from the sunflower family (Asteraceae). Gerbera is also commonly known as the African Daisy. Gerbera is also important commercially. It is the fifth most used cut flower in the world (after rose, carnation, chrysanthemum, and tulip).

41.didYouKnow: Lilium (members of which are true lilies) is a genus of herbaceous flowering plants growing from bulbs, all with large prominent flowers. Most species are native to the temperate northern hemisphere, though their range extends into the northern subtropics.

42.didYouKnow: Jute is a long, soft, shiny vegetable fiber that can be spun into coarse, strong threads. It is produced from plants in the genus Corchorus. Jute is the name of the plant or fiber that is used to make burlap, Hessian or gunny cloth.

43.didYouKnow: Melaleuca uncinata, commonly known as Broombush, Broom Honeymyrtle or Brushwood, is a plant in the Paperbark family native to southern Australia. It is harvested from the wild, and grown in plantations, for broombush fencing.

44.didYouKnow: Every breath you take will act as a reminder that your pungent pal is right behind you. Use item to acquire the Garlic Jester minion.

45.didYouKnow: Though wed to the Tomato King in a move to strengthen the bond of Houses Tomato and Mandragora, the queen longs to steal away with her one true love, the Eggplant Knight. What will become of this forbidden romance!? Use item to acquire the Mandragora Queen minion and find out!

46.didYouKnow: The onion (Allium cepa), also known as the bulb onion or common onion, is used as a vegetable and is the most widely cultivated species of the genus Allium. Onions contain phenolics and flavonoids that have potential anti-inflammatory, anti-cholesterol, anticancer and antioxidant properties.

47.didYouKnow: His childhood dream to become a wizard was frowned upon by his overbearing father. Use item to acquire the Eggplant Knight minion.

48.didYouKnow: The common fig (Ficus carica) is a species of flowering plant in the genus Ficus, from the family Moraceae. Native to the Middle East and western Asia, it has been sought out and cultivated since ancient times, and is now widely grown throughout the temperate world, both for its fruit and as an ornamental plant.

49.didYouKnow: A nut is a fruit composed of a hard shell and a seed, which is generally edible. In botanical jargon, there is an additional requirement that the shell does not open to release the seed. Most seeds come from fruits that naturally free themselves from the shell, unlike nuts such as hazelnuts, chestnuts, and acorns, which have hard shell walls and originate from a compound ovary.

50.didYouKnow: Has issued a royal decree for the public defenestration of any and all who mistakenly claim the king is a vegetable. Use item to acquire the King Tomato minion.

51.didYouKnow: bitter carrot-like root vegetable native to the Near East. While typically avoided by the five races, it is a favorite among chocobos.

52.didYouKnow: He would die 4 U. Or possibly cry 4 U. Or most likely make U cry. 4 U. Use item to acquire the Onion Prince minion.

53.didYouKnow: The potato is a starchy, tuberous crop from the perennial nightshade Solanum tuberosum. The potato was originally believed to have been domesticated independently in multiple locations, but later genetic testing of the wide variety of cultivars and wild species proved a single origin for potatoes in the area of present-day southern Peru and extreme northwestern Bolivia, where they were domesticated approximately 7,000-10,000 years ago.

54.didYouKnow: The potato is a starchy, tuberous crop from the perennial nightshade Solanum tuberosum. The potato was originally believed to have been domesticated independently in multiple locations, but later genetic testing of the wide variety of cultivars and wild species proved a single origin for potatoes in the area of present-day southern Peru and extreme northwestern Bolivia, where they were domesticated approximately 7,000-10,000 years ago.

55.didYouKnow: Allium sativum, commonly known as garlic, is a species in the onion genus, Allium. Its close relatives include the onion, shallot, leek, chive, and rakkyo. With a history of human use of over 7,000 years, garlic is native to central Asia,[3] and has long been a staple in the Mediterranean region, as well as a frequent seasoning in Asia, Africa, and Europe.

56.didYouKnow: Ginger or ginger root is the rhizome of the plant Zingiber officinale, consumed as a delicacy, medicine, or spice. Ginger is indigenous to southern China, from whence it spread to the Spice Islands and other parts of Asia, and subsequently to West Africa and the Caribbean.

57.didYouKnow: The onion (Allium cepa), also known as the bulb onion or common onion, is used as a vegetable and is the most widely cultivated species of the genus Allium. Onions contain phenolics and flavonoids that have potential anti-inflammatory, anti-cholesterol, anticancer and antioxidant properties.

58.didYouKnow: A bitter fruit found growing in the Ixali homelands. While typically avoided by the five races, and even the Ixal themselves, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to deepen the red hues of their feathers.

59.didYouKnow: A hard, bitter fruit found in the forests of far eastern Doma. While typically avoided by the five races, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to lighten the red hues of their feathers.

60.didYouKnow: A bitter fruit found growing in the arid soils of Mamook. While typically avoided by the five races, and even the Mamool Ja themselves, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to deepen the green hues of their feathers.

61.didYouKnow: A bitter berry found growing on the distant Isle of Val. While typically avoided by the five races, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to lighten the green hues of their feathers.

62.didYouKnow: A bitter fruit found growing on the volcanic soil of O'Ghomoro. While typically avoided by the five races, and the kobolds themselves, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to deepen the blue hues of their feathers.

63.didYouKnow: A bitter fruit found growing on the Rhotano Sea's Cieldalaes islands. While typically avoided by the five races, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to lighten the blue hues of their feathers.

64.didYouKnow: A foul, bitter fruit found growing on the Near Eastern island of Thavnair. While typically avoided by the residents of Radz-at-Han, it is a favorite amongst chocobos, and is known to restore feathers to their original hue.

65.didYouKnow: A deep red variety of dragon pepper so hot it is widely believed by Far Eastern cultures to drive away malignant spirits.

66.didYouKnow: Ficus carica is an Asian species of flowering plants in the mulberry family, known as the common fig (or just the fig). It is the source of the fruit also called the fig and is now widely grown throughout the temperate world, both for its fruit and as an ornamental plant.

67.didYouKnow: Chives is the common name of Allium schoenoprasum. A perennial plant, it is widespread in nature across much of Europe, Asia and North America. Chives are a commonly used herb and have insect-repelling properties that can be used in gardens to control pests.

68.didYouKnow: Cabbage (Brassica oleracea or variants) is a leafy green or purple biennial plant, grown as an annual vegetable crop for its dense-leaved heads. Closely related to other cole crops, such as broccoli, cauliflower, and brussels sprouts, it descends from B. oleracea var. oleracea, a wild field cabbage.

69.didYouKnow: Tea is an aromatic beverage commonly prepared by pouring hot or boiling water over cured leaves of the tea plant, Camellia sinensis. After water, tea is the most widely consumed beverage in the world. Tea originated in China as a medicinal drink. It was first introduced to Portuguese priests and merchants in China during the 16th century.

70.didYouKnow: Lavandula (common name lavender) is a genus of 39 known species of flowering plants in the mint family, Lamiaceae. It is native to the Old World and is found from Cape Verde and the Canary Islands, southern Europe across to northern and eastern Africa, the Mediterranean, southwest Asia to southeast India.

71.didYouKnow: Given its bizarre shape and extreme toxicity, this root is thought to have originated from the void.

72.didYouKnow: A rose is a woody perennial of the genus Rosa, within the family Rosaceae. Most species are native to Asia, with smaller numbers native to Europe, North America, and northwest Africa.

79.didYouKnow: Viola typically have heart-shaped, scalloped leaves and most lack any noticeable stems and the foliage and flowers appear to rise from the ground

80.didYouKnow: An oak is a tree or shrub native to the Northern Hemisphere, and includes deciduous and evergreen species extending from cool temperate to tropical latitudes in the Americas, Asia, Europe, and North Africa.

81.didYouKnow: The cherry fruits of commerce usually are obtained from a limited number of species such as cultivars of the sweet cherry, Prunus avium. The name 'cherry' also refers to the cherry tree, and is sometimes applied to almonds and visually similar flowering trees as in ornamental cherry or cherry blossom.

82.didYouKnow: Daisy is one of the flower names that burst back into bloom after a century's hibernation. Originally a nickname for Margaret (the French Marguerite is the word for the flower), Daisy comes from the phrase day's eye because it opens its petals at daybreak.

83.didYouKnow: Lilium (members of which are true lilies) is a genus of herbaceous flowering plants growing from bulbs, all with large prominent flowers. Most species are native to the temperate northern hemisphere, though their range extends into the northern subtropics.

84.harvestLocation: 僅限雜交／飛空艇探索（50級）
84.didYouKnow: Lack of imagination has led to most all white flowers found growing in the skybound regions of Dravania and Abalathia to be named after clouds, this specimen featuring wing-like petals being no exception.

85.didYouKnow: A fern is a member of a group of vascular plants (plants with xylem and phloem) that reproduce via spores and have neither seeds nor flowers. Ferns are not of major economic importance, but some are used for food, medicine, as biofertilizer, as ornamental plants and for remediating contaminated soil.

86.didYouKnow: Tulips (Tulipa) form a genus of spring-blooming perennial herbaceous bulbiferous geophytes (having bulbs as storage organs). The flowers are usually large, showy and brightly coloured, generally red yellow or white.

87.didYouKnow: Dahlia is a genus of bushy, tuberous, herbaceous perennial plants native to Mexico. There are 42 species of dahlia, with hybrids commonly grown as garden plants. Flower forms are variable, with one head per stem; these can be as small as 5 cm (2 in) diameter or up to 30 cm (1 ft).

88.didYouKnow: A native of Europe, Arum Italicum is grown for its unusual hooded flower, similar to a Jack in the Pulpit. The attractive foliage is arrow shaped, sometimes generously mottled in silvery white, and appears in fall, persisting through winter even when covered with snow.

89.didYouKnow: Lily of the valley is a sweetly scented, highly poisonous woodland flowering plant that is native throughout the cool temperate Northern Hemisphere in Asia and Europe.

90.didYouKnow: Hydrangea is a genus of 70–75 species of flowering plants native to Asia and the Americas. ‘Hydrangea’ is derived from Greek and means ‘water vessel’, which is in reference to the shape of its seed capsules.

91.didYouKnow: Campanula is one of several genera in the family Campanulaceae with the common name bellflower. It takes both its common and its scientific name from its bell-shaped flowers—campanula is Latin for "little bell".

92.didYouKnow: Hyacinthus is a small genus of bulbous, fragrant flowering plants in the family Asparagaceae, subfamily Scilloideae. These are commonly called hyacinths. The genus is native to the eastern Mediterranean.

93.didYouKnow: Capable of movement and tremendously juicy.

94.didYouKnow: These seeds will grow into bright and cheery cosmos blooms?unless one is a terrible gardener, in which case they will grow into a sense of self-loathing.

95.didYouKnow: Carnation or clove pink, is a species of Dianthus. It is probably native to the Mediterranean region but its exact range is unknown due to extensive cultivation for the last 2,000 years.

96.didYouKnow: Orchids in the genus Phalaenopsis are monopodial epiphytic, sometimes lithophytic herbs with long, coarse roots and short leafy stems hidden by overlapping leaf bases.

97.didYouKnow: Triteleia is a genus of monocotyledon flowering plants also known as triplet lilies. They get their name from the fact that all parts of their flowers come in threes.

98.didYouKnow: They say it is by Byregot's very hand that these flowers are coaxed to blossom...but you should still water them nevertheless.

99.didYouKnow: The sweet pea is a flowering plant in the genus Lathyrus in the family Fabaceae, native to Sicily, southern Italy and the Aegean Islands.

100.didYouKnow: Most morning glory flowers unfurl into full bloom in the early morning. The flowers usually start to fade a few hours before the corolla begins to display visible curling.

101.didYouKnow: Chrysanthemums, sometimes called mums or chrysanths, are flowering plants of the genus Chrysanthemum in the family Asteraceae. They are native to East Asia and northeastern Europe. Most species originate from East Asia and the center of diversity is in China.

102.didYouKnow: Lupine flowers may be annual and last only for a season, or perennial, returning for a few years in the same spot in which they were planted. The lupine plant grows from a long taproot and does not like to be moved. The genus includes over 199 species, with centers of diversity in North and South America.

103.didYouKnow: The common sunflower is a species of large annual forb of the daisy family Asteraceae. The common sunflower is harvested for its edible oily seeds which are used in the production of cooking oil.

104.didYouKnow: Native to Costa Rica and much of South America, this flamboyant, many colored orchid was extremely popular in flower arrangements (especially for corsages, as its name implies) in the 1950s.

105.didYouKnow: An evergreen, climbing shrub that usually grows 3–3.5 m (10–12 ft) tall, occasionally up to 9 m (30 ft). It features thick, thorny stems and drooping branches that are glabrous or sparsely hairy.

106.didYouKnow: Magnolia champaca, known in English as champak, is a large evergreen tree in the family Magnoliaceae. It is known for its fragrant flowers, and its timber used in woodworking.

107.didYouKnow: Flowering tea consists of a bundle of dried tea leaves wrapped around one or more dried flowers. Flowering tea in its current form was developed in China in the 1980s, and first became popular in Western countries in the early 2000s.
